sentences.text: Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we used-- when I used to live in the ground, I made a-- I made a big bunker, a big bunker.
Because we were about 10, 12 people.
And my father gave me to cover at night-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do they call this?
What do they call this?
A blanket, yes.
There was goose feathers.
And I used to cover myself.
And some boys covered-- help cover them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We used to live very tired.
Everything was cold.
Everything was cold, till the springtime come.
I used to go with a gun-- with a gun.
Give me a bread.
And they were afraid.
The Polish people were afraid.
They used to give me bread.
I want something to-- meat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We want to find the place where the cows are.
We used to take out a cow, a little cow, and take him in the woods, and kill them.
We used to cook.
In the daytime, I used to go out and make fire, and cook the meat.
No.
Yeah, they did.
They did find me.
Several times, several people find me.
Or I told them-- I used to go around with a gun and told them, if you want to call the German, I'm going to come and kill everyone in your family.
Which is true.
I-- we used to do it.
Oh, they didn't tell nobody.
They didn't [? do. ?] The Polish, they were very rotten.
They used to talk about the-- the Jews live in-- it was [INAUDIBLE] they lived there.
Oh, they didn't run over to the Germans to tell the Germans how there-- over there live Jews.
No, they didn't.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I used to go for food, we had plenty food.
They give us plenty food.
And when the time, when the German came and killed all the family, they came to me too.
Because that's, you know, there was a way go.
We run away.
We run about 20 miles away.
They didn't caught us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we stayed there till it became dark.
And we run to a-- in a Polish place where they-- people used to keep the corn and the wheat.
We sleeped over there.
Well, the Polack knows we came-- we live there.
He came fast to run.
Germans surrendered.
He didn't came when they were close.
I don't know he find out, but they were a couple miles away.
And we run away.
We run away, and they shoot at us.
We run about four miles away.
They shoot on us bullets, so fast.
They shoot on us.
They didn't caught us, didn't caught us.
Nobody was killed.
Nobody.
We run away to the same place where we used to stay.
We find a little girl who was frozen.
She frozen up.
She run away from the-- from the bunker, with her father, and the mother lived there.
She run to us.
But we didn't was there.
And she frozen up.
We took her, and we buried her with the father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
All right.
We lived.
I lived to the time a Russian man came over.
He said to me, who knows the road how to go to-- to Russian army?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, I know.
You know why I know?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Before the war started, my father rented a place in a different city, with-- with-- with a water, by the brook-- by the brook.
And he made his shop over there, and I used to be there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he was just such a good blacksmith.
He used to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
He used to take-- He went to a Polish man, and pay him $50.
They should make for him a wagon.
You know what's mean a wagon?
Mm-hm.
Woods to make a wagon.
And he made it iron work.
That-- my father made iron work.
And the Polish man gave him the wood.
And I know the guy.
I used to go to.
I said to the-- to the Russian guy.
He says to me, the Russian guy says to me, there's a hundred feet water.
We have to-- how we going to go the other side with the Russian?
Because we have to go past the water.
I says, I know somebody.
They have a boat.
And he's gonna give us the boat.
And we gonna swim to the other side where the Russians are.
And I went with him, with the Russian guy, to this-- this Polish man.
He recognized me.
He didn't-- was afraid to give it to me, because he knows already who I am.
He gave it me, the boat.
And we run away to the Russian side.
All-- it was still German there, not too far, even by a couple miles, the other side.
They shoot on us.
They didn't caught us.
They didn't kill us.
No.
And I went to the Russian army.
Yeah.
Yes, we lived very nice, very close.
For example, we used to go for food.
I didn't go myself.
I took from these people, five, six people.
And we went together, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for bread, for meat, for potatoes.
We lived together whole time, a very good-- good-- what do they call in English?
Good--
Friends, yeah, very good friends.
And when, you know, when I said-- when I went with the Russian way, it's true.
It's my own fault.
I should tell my brother-in-law which he come with me too.
I didn't tell nobody.
Because, you know, they have their own mind.
If they wanted to go, they don't want to go.
Because the war has been finishing already, the end the war.
Because the Russian come close to us.
They thought they going to hide themself, and they gonna survive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
I came-- ?] There was a man, two brothers lived together.
They were of a Polish family.
They lived with them about six, seven month.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this Polish family they thought-- the Polish family thought-- thought they have a lot of money, these two boys.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] One, the name was [?
Yosef. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other name was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [? forgot ?] [? the name. ?] I don't remember it.
They-- they gave him-- there was a father and a son, which they lived with them.
The two boys lived with them.
They gave them the saw to cut wood.
And the father and the son kept the axe.
You know, the axe?
And then when they gave them the saw to saw the-- the trees, the father and the son chopped it, the two boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the axe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The boy-- the younger boy, they killed him.
The axe was sharp.
And they killed him.
And the older one, he came to us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The bandage on his head, they didn't kill him.
He came to us.
He was with us till July.
In July, he says, no.
I have to go kill the man, kill the people who they killed my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They lived in Dubica.
Dubica when there was [NON-ENGLISH], woods-- that was about 10 miles away.
Their own ideas.
Yeah.
I never told him to do this, because I was afraid myself.
If I gonna do such a thing, they gonna to spite and to look for me.
They gonna kill me.
No, I didn't say nothing.
I said, [?
Yosef, ?] you have to do it yourself.
Do you know, I'm gonna go with you.
I'm not gonna shoot them.
I'm gonna come with you.
I'm gonna stay by the window.
And you breaker in the door.
And do anything you want.
He did it.
I was there, yeah.
They broke in-- in the house through the door.
And he killed the young fella and his wife.
Yeah, I see it.
I see it clearly.
All right.
When they killed-- he killed them, he went to the father.
The father, he couldn't kill.
He jumped out of the window.
He run away.
Couldn't catch him.
He run away.
He-- we run back to our place where we used to live.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] That's the one-- that's the father.
He survived.
He run away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He went over, they-- they-- not everybody want me-- me the Russian-- to the Russian army.
Three people.
Three.
We went to the army.
But we survived.
Survived.
Very nice.
[LAUGHS] That's a funny thing.
You know, I became a-- a Red Cross.
You never seen Red Cross?
Yeah?
It's a funny thing.
I was a young fella.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the father, I want to tell the father, organized more people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they [? went ?] [? over ?] to kill everyone-- everyone one of them, Jews.
They were in the [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE] in a barn.
They were sleeping, because the German was close by.
They hiding themselves.
And that's the one, he organized more people.
And they kill all the Jews, which they were with me, together.
Yes, they were.
Yeah.
How I find out?
In the-- I was liberated in the Russians.
I went to the army.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to the army.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's a sad story, I tell.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We pass by the city, which I used to live.
And the Polish people told me that the Polacks killed one by one.
When I went to the army, to the Russian army in to fight the German, I was wounded, wounded.
Yeah, I tell.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The bullets went through my foot.
It didn't went through the foot, it went to this one.
[INAUDIBLE] And I saw I'm in trouble.
I take a-- a razor, cut it the foot.
It let me blood.
The Russians should take me away from the front.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took me to the-- to bandage.
To-- what do they call this?
Yeah.
Yeah.
To-- and I went to the-- what you call-- the first aid.
And they wrapped around me.
I was in the front, not far from the brook, which they supposed to be liberated.
The-- the boys, who they were with me, they said that we not going no place.
We gonna be liberated from the Russian army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And they bandaged me in the foot.
There was not too much.
I said, you mental sick.
You go-- I live.
I survived such a Hitler bomb time.
You want to send me back to the army?
Oh, no.
I'm not going to go.
Kill me.
I'm not gonna go.
And who did you tell that to?
I told it to the-- to the Russian people, to the Russian-- to the officers who they send it back to the army.
Oh, no.
I'm not gonna go.
You could kill me now.
I'm gonna go.
They saw I managed, and they made me a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know a [NON-ENGLISH]?
You know, a cook, [LAUGHS] by the officers' kitchen.
They made me a cook.
And I used to put-- make shoes, and make for the horses shoes, and fix the wagons, and do-- they loved me.
You know, they loved me.
And I was with them about a year and a half, till the war ended.
The war ended.
I said I wanted to go back to home.
I wanna go home.
I'm not a Russian.
Which I supposed to stay a Russian.
I want to go back home.
Maybe somebody would survive and I'm gonna live with them.
They let me go home.
They let me.
And I went home with a train.
I went home.
And I find my girlfriend, which she survived.
And we was getting married.
Hele.
Hele.
I gave you her name.
And we got married.
We-- we-- I got married.
I didn't even was happy.
Not too-- not too happy.
The thing is, you know, you have to, like they say, look away a lot of things.
You know.
She has a survivor too, like I was a survivor.
She was a very nervous wreck.
I was a quiet boy.
You know, we want to be rejoicing.
We got married.
We run away.
The Polish people, came to the-- to the Polacks.
They kill a few Jews.
Yes.
They wanted send me to the Polish army, and I didn't want to go.
I run away.
I run to-- to-- what do you call this?
You know.
What do they call this?
To liberate-- liberate from the-- from the-- all soldiers.
Oh, that was in-- in-- that was 1940.
'44 I was liberated.
I went home when I was liberated.
I didn't was happy married with this woman.
You know, I was a live boy.
You know, a boy with full-- a lot of life-- life, you know?
All right, listen.
I was with her about [? 50 ?] years.
Tell me what happened.
Did you go back to your hometown immediately after?
No, no.
I didn't.
No.
I thought I saw a Russian guy, a Russian.
He had a taxi.
And I told him, please, could you take me to my home?
I wanted see something there.
If he is going there, he was going the same city where I was living then.
And my father buried a lot of gold in a-- in a box.
And I-- he showed me-- my father showed me.
Somebody survive, they should go take a-- open-- take a-- dig it out and take the gold, which I-- I did that.
And I went over there.
And I picked-- picked the gold out.
There was chains, and a clock, and all kinds, rings, all kind rings.
You know.
And my wife has it now.
She has it.
The chains is, for example, you have to go around twice, gold, 14 karat gold.
And one-- one chain is 18 karat gold.
And I took it off.
[LAUGHS]
In-- I didn't go to my hometown.
I went to Parczew-- Parczew.
It's in another town.
And the Polish people came and killed some Jews.
They killed.
That's right.
I left.
I left, and-- and I went back to the other guy with my wife, to stay in the other city.
We stayed there about two, three weeks.
Later we run.
We move to Bratslav.
They killed some Jewish people.
And I-- I and a few more Jewish people went in-- what do you call?
The horses were downstairs.
And on the top, we went there, and they couldn't find us.
Oh, they went into our house.
And they took away-- we used to have business with material.
They took away all the material.
They took it out.
And they were looking for us.
And they didn't caught us.
And we survived.
